narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 56.8% chance that you would not pass—crossing the threshold we use to make admission decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.20 and LSAT score of 35, alongside your Year 1 and Year 3 grade deciles, family income in the middle bracket, and your plan to attend full-time. The model weighed these factors against patterns from thousands of past students, focusing on those most predictive of bar success. The strongest influence pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking of 2, which stands well below the average of about 6 for students who passed the bar, signaling to the model a notable dip in performance relative to peers who succeeded. Next in importance was your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, which, though close to the 3.24 average among bar passers, pulled slightly in your favor by showing solid prior academic strength. Your Year 1 decile of 2 also contributed toward the failure prediction, again lower than the typical 6 for successful students, reinforcing concerns about early consistency. Compared to admitted students, who often come from a mix of family income levels but skew toward upper-middle brackets and boast stronger decile rankings and LSAT scores around 37, your profile aligned more closely with those who struggled on the bar. Your full-time enrollment plan matches the norm for passers (over 93%), offering a small positive note, while your middle-income background falls within the common range but didn't outweigh the academic signals. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—gender and race weren't factors here—but a data-driven flag on bar passage odds. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or above, and focus on strategies to improve your class rankings, perhaps through tutoring or study groups tailored to law school rigor. Many students in similar positions have strengthened their profiles this way and succeeded later. You have the drive; channeling it into these targeted improvements could shift the model's view dramatically next time. Keep pursuing your goals—we're rooting for your success.
